FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes data from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Data Release 5.0, n=7471 participants aged 9.87 years from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed-effect models (LME) on ABCD neuroimaging and lifestyle data; reports β-coefficients and mediation AMEs.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Translational Psychiatry (2025); no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen use appears in mediation analyses (β=-0.509 fluid intelligence; β=-0.699 crystallized intelligence) but the screen use variable is defined as 'the average hours an adolescent used screen in a day' from the ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire (parent report) — a generic multi-device aggregate measure, not a phone/SM-specific variable. No separate phone-specific or SM-specific estimate with its own coefficient exists anywhere in the paper. Step 2: The composite is not exclusively phone/SM items; it is an undifferentiated daily screen hours measure bundling all device types. Fails IC2. Additionally, screen use appears only as a mediator (lifestyle → MRI → intelligence pathway), not as IV or DV with its own primary coefficient.

notes: Screen use is measured as average daily screen hours (ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire, parent report) — a generic aggregate. It functions as a mediator variable in the lifestyle-intelligence mediation pathway. No phone-specific or SM-specific variable is reported anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from 11,875 U.S. ABCD Study participants (NDA Release 3.0); 8,125 included after QC; baseline ages 9-11 recruited from 21 sites across the United States.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using generalized additive mixed models and linear mixed-effects models on ABCD neuroimaging and behavioral data; reports β-coefficients throughout.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Scientific Reports (2025); no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate exists anywhere in the paper. The two exposures are (1) daily hours of pleasurable reading (books) and (2) daily hours of television viewing. Television viewing is explicitly TV/movies consumption — not smartphone, social media, texting, app use, or any phone/SM-specific activity. No separate phone or social media variable appears in any table, supplement, or sensitivity analysis. Step 2: Not applicable; neither exposure is a composite containing phone/SM items. The paper examines TV viewing and reading only — neither qualifies under FT-IC2.

notes: Paper exclusively examines reading and television viewing habits in relation to neurocognition and brain morphology. No phone, smartphone, social media, texting, or app-use variable appears anywhere.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes prospective longitudinal cohort data from the U.S. ABCD Study (Data Release 5.1; 2016-2022), n=6,779 adolescents aged 9-10 at baseline recruited from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical secondary analysis using mixed-effects linear modeling on ABCD data from baseline through 3-year follow-up; reports regression coefficients (b), 95% CIs, and p-values.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Issues in Mental Health Nursing (2025); no duplicate identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: PSMU (Problematic Social Media Use) measured via the SMAQ (6-item Social Media Addiction Questionnaire) is a qualifying SM variable (problematic/addictive SM scale). It appears as the dependent variable (DV) with its own reported coefficients throughout the paper (e.g., Table 2: High ACEs β=1.5, p<0.001 predicting SMAQ score at Y3). The other side of the relationship is ACEs (adverse childhood experiences / trauma exposure), which qualifies as individual-trait-level. FT-IC2 = MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Inferential mixed-effects linear regression reported throughout; Table 2 reports b-coefficients, 95% CIs, and p-values for ACEs → PSMU relationship (e.g., High ACEs: b=1.49, 95% CI 1.09-1.88, p=0.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: regression coefficients (b), 95% confidence intervals, and p-values for ACEs → PSMU association and moderation models in Tables 2 and 3.

FT_EC7_reason: All three FT-EC7 conditions are met: (a) longitudinal design — ACEs measured at Y1 (ages 10-12) predict PSMU at Y3 (ages 12-14); (b) SM/PSMU is the DV in every analysis — ACEs and BPM dysregulation are predictors/moderators, PSMU is always the outcome; (c) no analysis anywhere in the paper uses SM/PSMU as IV. No cross-sectional analysis with SM as DV exists anywhere in the paper (cross-sectional carve-out does not apply). FT-EC7 applies → EXCLUDE.
notes: Study is purely longitudinal: ACEs (IV) at Y1 predicting PSMU (DV) at Y3, with BPM dysregulation as moderator. PSMU/SMAQ is always the outcome. No SM-as-IV analysis and no cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis anywhere. EC7 applies.
